A fearful accident had occurred; not a collision, but a train had gone off the line, and then there had been a fall. The affair recalled the worst disasters of American railways. The river crossed by the railway was full of broken carriages and the engine. Whether the weight of the train had been too much for the bridge, or whether the train had gone off the rails, the fact remained that five carriages out of six fell into the bed of the Loddon, dragged down by the locomotive. The sixth carriage, miraculously preserved by the breaking of the coupling chain, remained on the rails, six feet from the abyss. Below nothing was discernible but a melancholy heap of twisted and blackened axles, shattered wagons, bent rails, charred sleepers; the boiler, burst by the shock, had scattered its plates to enormous distances. From this shapeless mass of ruins flames and black smoke still rose. After the fearful fall came fire, more fearful still! Great tracks of blood, scattered limbs, charred trunks of bodies, showed here and there; none could guess how many victims lay dead and mangled under those ruins.

[OC-036-013]Another most important contribution to the magnificence of the nights upon Saturn is the triple ring with which, as a brilliant setting, the planet is encompassed. To an observer at the equator, this ring, which has been estimated by Sir William Herschel as scarcely 100 miles in thickness, must have the appearance of a narrow band of light passing through the zenith 12,000 miles above his head. As the observer, however, increases his latitude either north or south, the band will gradually widen out into three detached and concentric rings, of which the innermost, dark though transparent, is 9,625 miles in breadth; the intermediate one, which is brighter than the planet itself, being 17,605 miles broad; and the outer, of a dusky hue, being 8,660 miles broad.

Powerful as were her machines, the Caucasus could not thus, after entering the Kama, make against the current more than ten miles an hour. They measured up to fifteen meters in height over a length of 150 to 175 meters, and the speed of their propagation (half that of the wind) was fifteen meters per second. Their volume and power increased with the depth of the waters. I then understood the role played by these waves, which trap air in their flanks and release it in the depths of the sea where its oxygen brings life. Their utmost pressure--it has been calculated-- can build to 3,000 kilograms on every square foot of surface they strike. It was such waves in the Hebrides that repositioned a stone block weighing 84,000 pounds. If its speed had been enormous, he would have understood why it had not fallen. But with a relatively slight speed the resistance to lunar attraction could not be explained. Was the projectile under the influence of some strange force? Did some body maintain it in the ether? It was henceforth evident that it would not touch any point upon the moon. Where was it going? description: 'The Italian volcano through whose crater the explorers are expelled at the end of Journey to the Center of the Earth.'

Stromboli is the site of the expedition's violent emergence. Carried upward on a rising column of lava, the party comes "out of the crater half naked" (JCE-044-010), saved from the fire at the last moment. Above them they see "the crater of a volcano" through which they have been expelled (JCE-044-016), the vent that has just vomited them onto the mountainside.

label: The Fur Country

Cape Bathurst is the Arctic promontory on the North American coast that Lieutenant Jasper Hobson chooses as the site of a new Hudson's Bay Company factory in *The Fur Country*. The coast juts north-westward from the gulf, ending in Cape Bathurst, which lies above the seventieth parallel. (FC-010-008) It becomes the setting for the novel's central catastrophe.
label: Union Bay
- [[Captain Hatteras]] — commander who steers the Forward to the island
description: 'The fictional Antarctic land named by the shipwrecked crew of An Antarctic Mystery for their lost schooner, the Halbrane.'

The Aleutian Islands are the real chain forming the southern rim of Behring Sea, the last landfall the castaways of *The Fur Country* dare hope for. As Victoria Island drifts south, the group pins its survival on reaching "the Aleutian group."
# Washington Bay

The island lies within the treacherous coral maze of the Torres Strait, where "Dumont d'Urville's two sloops of war had gone aground." (TTLU-020-033) Its coast is fringed by the reef that briefly traps the Nautilus, and its interior yields breadfruit and game to the foraging Canadian.

The cape is the fixed reference from which the explorers reckon their drift across the Central Sea. "From Cape Saknussemm we had been carried due north for hundreds of leagues," (JCE-043-026) Axel records of the storm that seized their raft.

Nevada is a land of silver walled off by mountains. In [[Robur the Conqueror]] the [[Albatross]] came "in Nevada over the silver regions, which the Sierra separates from the golden lands of [[California]]" (RC-010-033) — the Sierra Nevada standing as the state's western rampart.

Through the observatory of Cambridge in Massachusetts it links the astronomical debates of *From the Earth to the Moon*. (FEM-004-003) And as one of the shores along which the Terror goes scurrying, it joins the New England coast of *The Master of the World*. (MW-006-025)
Elsewhere it figures as a theatre of military service in *Off on a Comet* and as a field of geographical research in *The Waif of the "Cynthia"*.
